steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Need to filter /var/log/app/access.log down to only requests that hit our internal API endpoints — anything matching `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/` — and dump those lines to /home/user/internal_hits.log. But here's the thing: we're also seeing a ton of health check spam from the load balancer that I want excluded, those come from 10.0.0.0/8 range and hit `/health` or `/ready`. So internal API calls yes, but not if they're from the 10.x.x.x health probes.

Also the timestamps in that log are in that annoying Apache combined format with the brackets, would be nice if the output had them as ISO 8601 instead (like 2024-01-15T14:30:22). Rest of the line can stay as-is.

Log's about 50k lines so nothing crazy but I don't want to sit here doing it by hand.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
user@8b89897c3e68:~$

Analysis: I'm at a fresh terminal prompt. I need to:
1. First check if the log file exists at /var/log/app/access.log
2. Examine its format to understand the structure
3. Filter for lines matching `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/`
4. Exclude lines from 10.0.0.0/8 IP range
5. Convert Apache timestamp format (like [15/Jan/2024:14:30:22 +0000]) to ISO 8601 (2024-01-15T14:30:22)
6. Write results to /home/user/internal_hits.log
Plan: First, I'll check if the log file exists and examine a few sample lines to understand its format. Then I'll craft a command pipeline using grep and awk/sed to filter and transform the data as needed.

results.content:
Previous response had warnings:
WARNINGS: - Extra text detected before JSON object

user@8b89897c3e68:~$ head -5 /var/log/app/access.log

34.28.48.212 - - [05/May/2024:10:23:56 +0000] "PATCH /api/v1/public/data HTTP/1.1" 400 5873 "-" "Python-urllib/3.9"
10.86.51.19 - - [27/Aug/2024:10:40:43 +0000] "GET /ready HTTP/1.1" 204 2407 "-" "ELB-HealthChecker/2.0"

user@8b89897c3e68:~$

steps.message:
Analysis: The log file exists (5.7M, about 50k lines as mentioned) and is in Apache combined log format. The format is: IP - - [timestamp] "method path protocol" status size "-" "user-agent". I can see examples of health checks from 10.x.x.x IPs hitting /ready, and some /api/v1/public/ endpoints. I need to find lines with /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ that don't come from 10.x.x.x IPs, and convert timestamps from [05/Dec/2024:10:07:16 +0000] to ISO 8601 format.
Plan: I'll look for some internal API examples first to confirm they exist, then build a command pipeline that:
1. Uses grep to filter for /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ patterns
2. Excludes lines starting with 10. (using grep -v or awk)
3. Uses awk/sed to convert the timestamp format from [DD/MMM/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +ZZZZ] to YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS
4. Writes output to /home/user/internal_hits.log

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

user@8b89897c3e68:~$ grep -E '/api/v[0-9]+/internal/' /var/log/app/access.log | head -3
192.168.32.122 - - [23/Aug/2024:03:38:50 +0000] "PATCH /api/v1/internal/status HTTP/1.1" 401 5184 "-" "Python-urllib/3.9"
198.170.22.163 - - [04/Apr/2024:08:16:59 +0000] "PATCH /api/v1/internal/status HTTP/1.1" 404 8876 "-" "PostmanRuntime/7.29.0"
52.190.55.116 - - [19/Jan/2024:12:49:14 +0000] "DELETE /api/v2/internal/users HTTP/1.1" 403 7811 "-" "WebhookClient/1.0"
grep: write error: Broken pipe
user@8b89897c3e68:~$

steps.message:
Analysis: Good! Internal API endpoints exist in the log. I can see /api/v1/internal/ and /api/v2/internal/ paths. Now I need to build the full pipeline to: 1) filter for internal API calls, 2) exclude 10.x.x.x IPs, and 3) convert timestamps. The timestamp conversion needs to map month names (Jan, Feb, etc.) to numbers and reformat from [DD/MMM/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +ZZZZ] to YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS.
Plan: I'll create a command pipeline using grep and awk. First grep for the internal API pattern, then use awk to: 1) skip lines starting with '10.', 2) convert the timestamp format, and 3) reconstruct the log line. I'll use awk's month name to number conversion and string manipulation for the timestamp transformation.

arguments.keystrokes: grep -E '/api/v[0-9]+/internal/' /var/log/app/access.log | awk 'BEGIN {month["Jan"]="01"; month["Feb"]="02"; month["Mar"]="03"; month["Apr"]="04"; month["May"]="05"; month["Jun"]="06"; month["Jul"]="07"; month["Aug"]="08"; month["Sep"]="09"; month["Oct"]="10"; month["Nov"]="11"; month["Dec"]="12"} !/^10\./ {match($0, /\[([0-9]{2})\/([A-Z][a-z]{2})\/([0-9]{4}):([0-9]{2}):([0-9]{2}):([0-9]{2}) [^]]+\]/, ts); newts=ts[3]"-"month[ts[2]]"-"ts[1]"T"ts[4]":"ts[5]":"ts[6]; sub(/\[[^]]+\]/, newts); print}' > /home/user/internal_hits.log

steps.message:
Analysis: The command executed successfully without errors. Now I need to verify that the output file was created correctly and check: 1) that it contains the filtered internal API calls, 2) that 10.x.x.x IPs were excluded, 3) that timestamps were converted to ISO 8601 format.
Plan: I'll check the output file by: 1) verifying it exists and checking its size, 2) looking at a few sample lines to confirm the timestamp conversion worked, 3) verifying no 10.x.x.x IPs are present, and 4) counting the total lines to ensure we got a reasonable number of results.

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! The output file was created successfully with 4,800 lines (564K). The timestamps have been correctly converted to ISO 8601 format (e.g., 2024-08-23T03:38:50). All lines shown are internal API endpoints (/api/v1/internal/, /api/v2/internal/, /api/v3/internal/). Now I need to verify that 10.x.x.x IPs were properly excluded.
Plan: I'll verify that no 10.x.x.x IP addresses are in the output file by grepping for lines starting with '10.'. I should get no results. I'll also do a final sanity check to confirm the filtering worked correctly.

arguments.keystrokes: echo "Verification: Lines with 10.x.x.x IPs in output (should be 0): $(grep '^10\.' /home/user/internal_hits.log | wc -l)"

steps.message:
Analysis: Excellent! The task has been completed successfully. Verification shows:
1. ✓ Output file created at /home/user/internal_hits.log (564K, 4,800 lines)
2. ✓ Only internal API endpoints matching /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ are included (v1, v2, v3 variants visible)
3. ✓ All 10.x.x.x IP addresses have been excluded (0 lines found with that pattern)
4. ✓ Timestamps successfully converted from Apache format [DD/MMM/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +ZZZZ] to ISO 8601 format YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS
5. ✓ Rest of the log line format preserved (IP, method, path, status, size, user-agent, etc.)

The tail output shows various internal API endpoints from different IP ranges (203.x, 34.x, 52.x, 192.168.x, 172.x) - all non-10.x IPs as required.
Plan: Task is complete. All requirements have been met: filtered for internal API endpoints, excluded health check traffic from 10.0.0.0/8, converted timestamps to ISO 8601, and saved to the specified output file.
